name: IPB (Intelligence Preparation of the Battlefield)
year_developed: 1989 (FM 34-130 first published as the definitive IPB manual); refined through successive revisions (FM 2-01.3 / JP 2-01.3 for joint IPB); roots in pre-WWII terrain analysis and enemy order of battle methodology
progenitor: US Army Intelligence Center of Excellence (Fort Huachuca) and TRADOC — institutional evolution. The methodology draws on WWII-era terrain analysis practices, Cold War Soviet threat modeling, and lessons from Vietnam. FM 34-130 (1989) is the landmark codification. JP 2-01.3 extended IPB to the joint level as JIPOE (Joint Intelligence Preparation of the Operational Environment) incorporating PMESII analysis
driving_insight: In military operations, the environment — terrain, weather, civilian populations, and the enemy — is not a neutral backdrop but an active determinant of success or failure. Commanders and planners who fail to systematically understand the environment before planning will produce plans that are defeated not primarily by the enemy but by terrain they didn't analyze, weather they didn't account for, or civil factors they didn't consider. The central insight of IPB is that systematic, disciplined, continuous intelligence preparation prevents the 'unknown unknowns' that produce operational surprise. By structurally decomposing the environment into analyzable components (terrain via OCOKA, weather effects, threat via doctrinal templates) and integrating them into a coherent picture of how the enemy will operate, IPB transforms raw intelligence into actionable understanding. The deeper contribution is moving intelligence from 'what is the enemy doing?' to 'what will the enemy do, where, when, and how?' — predictive intelligence that enables proactive rather than reactive decision-making.
decision_style: Deliberative and staff-driven. IPB is an analytical framework conducted by the intelligence staff (S2/G2) with input from all staff sections. The S2 leads the IPB process, but the operations officer (S3), engineer, fire support, and civil affairs officers all contribute their functional expertise. The commander's role is to: (a) provide initial guidance that focuses the IPB effort (what is most important to understand?); (b) approve key IPB products (modified combined obstacle overlay, enemy COA models); and (c) designate Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs) that emerged from IPB. IPB is the analytical foundation for MDMP/JOPP — it provides the 'understand the environment' piece that precedes COA development. The decision style is fundamentally data-driven: IPB builds models based on observable evidence (terrain analysis, weather data, enemy order of battle) and doctrinal templates, and the conclusions must be traceable to evidence.
problem_class_targeted: Well-defined to moderately ill-defined tactical, operational, and strategic military problems where the physical and human terrain is analyzable. IPB is designed for problems where: (a) the environment has significant physical, military, and civil dimensions that constrain and enable operations; (b) the adversary is a structured military force with doctrine, equipment, and patterns that can be modeled; (c) terrain and weather effects are predictable and can be analyzed in advance; (d) the friendly commander has planning time to conduct systematic analysis. It is less suited to: purely cyber/space operations where traditional terrain analysis is inapplicable; adversaries with no doctrinal templates (novel threats); highly dynamic environments where the IPB products become obsolete faster than they can be produced; and strategic problems where the political/diplomatic dimensions dominate over the military/terrain dimensions (though JIPOE partially addresses this with PMESII).
ordered_phases: IPB consists of four principal steps conducted iteratively for each mission. Step 1 — Define the Battlefield Environment: Identify the significant characteristics of the battlefield that will influence COAs and command decisions. Establish the Area of Operations (AO), Battlespace, and Area of Interest (AI). Identify the level of detail required vs. time available. Evaluate existing databases and identify intelligence gaps. Step 2 — Describe the Battlefield's Effects: Analyze terrain through OCOKA (Observation/Fields of Fire, Cover/Concealment, Obstacles, Key Terrain, Avenues of Approach). Analyze weather effects on both friendly and threat operations (visibility, winds, precipitation, cloud cover, temperature, humidity, moonlight). Analyze other relevant characteristics (population demographics, infrastructure, political/economic factors). Produce the Modified Combined Obstacles Overlay (MCOO). Step 3 — Evaluate the Threat: Update or create threat models (composition, disposition, tactics, training, readiness, logistics, electronic technical data). Create doctrinal templates depicting how the enemy doctrinally deploys and operates without terrain/weather constraints. Describe threat tactics and options narratively. Identify High-Value Targets (HVTs) — assets the enemy commander requires for mission success. Step 4 — Determine Threat Courses of Action: Identify the threat's likely objectives and desired end state. Identify the full set of COAs available to the threat. Evaluate and prioritize each COA for probability of adoption. Develop the most likely COA and most dangerous COA in detail. Produce situation templates (SITTEMPs — how the enemy deploys with terrain/weather constraints), event templates (time-phased predictions of critical events and target appearances), and the Decision Support Template (decision points keyed to battlefield events). Identify Named Areas of Interest (NAIs) and Target Areas of Interest (TAIs) to focus intelligence collection and targeting.
feedback_loops: IPB is explicitly designed as a continuous, iterative process — not a one-time analysis. The primary feedback loops are: (a) New intelligence triggers re-evaluation — when collection answers PIRs, the IPB products are updated and the refined understanding feeds back into planning; (b) Wargaming feedback — during COA analysis, wargaming tests the IPB's threat COA models against friendly COAs, and discrepancies trigger re-examination; (c) Execution feedback — enemy actions that deviate from predicted COAs trigger immediate IPB re-analysis ('the enemy is not doing what we expected — why?'); (d) Assumption validation — IPB products are built on assumptions that must be confirmed or denied; when denied, all products based on that assumption are re-evaluated. These loops are designed into the IPB-mission analysis-wargaming-execution cycle, making IPB a living analytical framework rather than a static baseline study.
gates: IPB has no formal internal gates — it is a continuous analytical process. The external gate is the Mission Analysis Brief (MDMP Gate 1), where the commander approves or modifies the staff's understanding of the environment (built on IPB products). If the commander rejects the IPB's enemy assessment (e.g., 'you've underestimated the enemy's air defense'), the S2 must re-analyze. Soft gates exist at: (a) the transition from Step 1 to Step 2 (is the environment adequately defined to analyze effects?); (b) the transition from Step 3 to Step 4 (are threat models sufficient to develop credible COAs?); (c) the production of key IPB products (MCOO, SITTEMP, DST) which are reviewed by the commander and staff.
exit_criteria: IPB is never truly 'done' — it is continuous throughout the operation. However, for a specific planning phase, IPB is 'sufficiently mature' when: (a) the environment is defined (AO, AI, significant characteristics); (b) terrain and weather effects on friendly and threat operations are analyzed and documented (MCOO produced); (c) threat models are current and credible (doctrinal templates, HVT list); (d) threat COAs are developed in sufficient detail to support COA analysis and wargaming (SITTEMP, event template, DST); and (e) intelligence gaps are identified and collection is tasked. The analysis is time-bound: when the planning timeline expires, IPB stops with whatever fidelity has been achieved, and residual unknowns are managed through assumptions and CCIRs.
decomposition_method: IPB decomposes the operational environment through a layered, domain-driven approach. The primary decomposition axes: (a) Spatial decomposition — the environment is decomposed into AO, battlespace, and AI, then further into terrain features (OCOKA), avenues of approach, engagement areas, and NAI/TAIs. (b) Environmental decomposition — the environment is decomposed into terrain (analyzed through OCOKA), weather (analyzed through effects on operations), civil considerations (ASCOPE for joint/multinational), and electromagnetic spectrum (for modern IPB). (c) Threat decomposition — the enemy is decomposed into order of battle (units, equipment, capabilities), then into doctrinal templates (how they fight), then into specific COAs (what they'll do in this environment). (d) Temporal decomposition — operations are decomposed into phases and events, with the event template predicting what happens when. The decomposition is domain-driven (each domain has its own analytical sub-framework) and hierarchical (from AO down to specific engagement areas). The IPB products — MCOO, SITTEMP, event template, DST — are the structured outputs of this decomposition.
granularity_guidance: IPB granularity is echelon-dependent. Division: AI up to 100 km / 72 hours time horizon; detailed terrain analysis at 1:50,000 to 1:100,000 scale. Brigade: AI up to 30 km / 24 hours; 1:25,000 to 1:50,000. Battalion: AI up to 15 km / 12 hours; 1:10,000 to 1:25,000. Company and below: most restrictive AI; terrain analysis at micro-terrain level (individual buildings, specific engagement areas). The stopping rule is pragmatic: analyze to the level of detail that affects the commander's decisions at this echelon. Beyond that, the detail is the responsibility of subordinate echelons conducting their own IPB. The time available determines depth — full IPB products when time permits; abbreviated MCOO plus critical threat COAs when time-constrained.
boundary_rules: IPB enforces analytical boundaries through its spatial and functional frameworks: (a) AO boundaries define the commander's responsibility — analysis focuses here; (b) AI boundaries define the intelligence collection requirement — analysis extends to wherever the threat can affect the AO within the planning horizon; (c) the MCOO integrates terrain obstacles and avenues of approach into a single coherent picture, ensuring no terrain gap is missed; (d) the doctrinal template ensures threat analysis is systematically organized by enemy unit type and echelon; (e) the distinction between most likely COA and most dangerous COA ensures the staff plans for both the expected and the worst case. Boundary conflicts (areas where terrain analysis doesn't match between adjacent units, or where threat assessments differ) are resolved through staff coordination. The IPB process provides the analytical framework for detecting and resolving these boundary issues before they affect operational planning.
risk_assessment_mechanism: IPB is a primary input to operational risk assessment but does not itself score or quantify risk. Risk factors identified by IPB include: (a) threat capabilities that can defeat the mission (identified in Step 3 and mapped in Step 4 COAs); (b) terrain that favors the enemy (identified in Step 2 — key terrain, defensible terrain, covered avenues of approach); (c) weather windows that constrain friendly operations; (d) civil factors that complicate operations (population density, political sensitivities); (e) intelligence gaps — unknowns that represent risk because the planning assumption could be wrong. These risk factors feed into the MDMP's wargaming and COA comparison steps where they are formally assessed. The DST embeds risk triggers: specific enemy actions or events that signal that risk is materializing. IPB's primary risk contribution is making the threat visible — the most dangerous risk is the threat you haven't analyzed.
unknown_handling: IPB systematically identifies, prioritizes, and addresses unknowns. Step 1 identifies intelligence gaps in existing databases. Steps 2-4 produce intelligence requirements that are prioritized: the most critical become Priority Intelligence Requirements (PIRs) — these are the unknowns that, if resolved, would change the commander's decision. Unknowns that cannot be resolved before execution become planning assumptions, explicitly stated in the OPORD: 'Enemy artillery estimated at 2-3 battalions southeast of Phase Line Blue. This is an assumption pending confirmation from SOF reconnaissance.' The IPB framework distinguishes between known facts (confirmed by intelligence), assessed judgments (inferred from evidence with stated confidence), and assumptions (treated as true for planning, to be validated). This explicitness about unknowns — rather than hiding them in confident-sounding assessments — is a core IPB discipline. The Joint variant (JIPOE) adds PMESII analysis to address unknowns across political, military, economic, social, information, and infrastructure dimensions.
default_stance_on_unknown: RESTRICTIVE — IPB defaults to conservative threat assessment. Enemy capabilities are assumed to exist until disproven. The most dangerous COA is always developed alongside the most likely COA, and planners must address both. Intel gaps are flagged as risks, not ignored. The conservative stance is driven by the stakes: underestimating the enemy is more dangerous than overestimating them. However, excessive conservatism leads to threat inflation (assuming the enemy has every possible capability) which can paralyze decision-making or waste resources. The commander calibrates the conservatism through planning guidance: 'I will accept the risk that the enemy does not have X capability; do not plan against it.' IPB provides the analytical basis for this calibration by distinguishing between what the enemy is known to have, what they are assessed to have, and what they could have in worst case.
safety_guardrails: IPB provides no operational safety mechanisms — it is an analytical framework. Safety guardrails for operations based on IPB reside in: (a) the rules of engagement (ROE); (b) the targeting process (collateral damage estimation, no-strike list); (c) the commander's acceptance of risk; (d) the law of armed conflict (LOAC). IPB contributes to safety indirectly by: (a) identifying civilian population concentrations (no-strike areas); (b) identifying protected sites (hospitals, schools, cultural sites); (c) analyzing civilian movement patterns to avoid collateral damage. The JIPOE variant's PMESII analysis explicitly incorporates civilian considerations that support operational safety and legitimacy.
human_decision_points: IPB is human-driven at every step. Key human decisions: (1) The commander's initial intelligence guidance that focuses the IPB effort. (2) The S2's analytical judgments — what terrain is 'key,' which avenue of approach is most dangerous, what the enemy's most likely COA is. (3) The commander's approval of IPB products — particularly the enemy COA models, which become the foundation for COA development and wargaming. (4) PIR designation — the commander decides which unknowns are decision-critical. (5) Continuous re-evaluation — during execution, the S2 and commander continuously assess whether the IPB is holding up against observed enemy behavior. The staff (S2/G2) is IN the loop for analysis; the commander is ON the loop for validation and decision.
override_capability: The commander can override any IPB analytical judgment — reject the S2's terrain assessment, disagree with the threat COA prioritization, or direct focus on a different avenue of approach. This override is the commander's prerogative based on personal experience and intuition. However, the commander's override must be documented — if the commander overrules the intelligence staff's assessment of the enemy's most likely COA and plans against a different threat, the rationale must be recorded. The S2 has the professional obligation to flag when the commander's direction conflicts with intelligence evidence — 'Sir, the intelligence does not support that enemy disposition' — but the commander makes the final decision.
fallback_when_no_human: Not applicable — IPB requires human analytical judgment to interpret terrain, model adversary behavior, and synthesize intelligence. In the LLM-agent context, automated IPB-equivalent analysis is feasible: the agent analyzes the codebase (terrain), technical constraints (weather), and obstacles (enemy) using structured analytical templates. The agent can produce a codebase map (MCOO equivalent), identify technical bottlenecks (key terrain), and predict where integration challenges will arise (enemy COA). However, the creative adversarial thinking required for credible threat COA development may exceed current LLM capabilities in genuinely novel problem spaces.
max_problem_size: Demonstrated at the theater strategic level. IPB has been applied to analyze operational environments ranging from a company patrol area (a few square kilometers) to an entire theater of operations (hundreds of thousands of square kilometers, as in Desert Storm or Iraqi Freedom). The joint variant (JIPOE) scales to global strategic analysis through PMESII. What breaks at scale: (a) the products become too large to be useful — a corps-level MCOO covering thousands of square kilometers loses the tactical detail needed by subordinate units; (b) the analysis cannot be maintained in real time — a theater-level IPB requires weeks to produce and is obsolete before completion if the environment is dynamic; (c) the threat COA analysis becomes too speculative — at theater scale, predicting enemy strategic behavior is inherently less reliable than predicting tactical behavior.
parallel_execution: IPB can be parallelized across staff sections: the S2 leads threat analysis (Steps 3-4); the engineer officer leads terrain analysis (Step 2); the S3 contributes to environment definition (Step 1) and the integration of terrain effects with operations; the civil affairs officer contributes to civil considerations; the air defense and aviation officers contribute to air IPB. At the joint level, component commands conduct parallel IPB for their domains (land, air, maritime, space, cyber) which the J2 integrates. Dependency resolution: threat COA development (Step 4) depends on terrain analysis (Step 2) and threat evaluation (Step 3), creating sequential dependencies. However, within Steps 2 and 3, multiple analysts can work in parallel on different terrain features, weather effects, or threat units.
team_scaling_model: IPB scales well with staff size because it provides natural analytical division across functional domains. A battalion S2 section (2-4 personnel) can produce adequate IPB for battalion operations. A division G2 section (20-50 personnel with attached intelligence assets) can produce detailed IPB with all formal products. An intelligence brigade or joint intelligence center (hundreds of analysts) can produce theater-level IPB and JIPOE with comprehensive PMESII analysis. Scaling is approximately linear: adding analysts enables parallel work on different terrain sectors, threat units, and functional domains. The limiting factor is integration — the G2/J2 must synthesize parallel analyses into coherent products, and the commander can only absorb so much analytical detail.
cost_scaling_model: In the military context, IPB cost is measured in intelligence staff-hours and collection resources tasked to fill intelligence gaps. Cost scales with: (a) the size of the AO/AI (larger areas require more terrain analysis); (b) the complexity of the enemy (more threat units require more doctrinal templates and COA models); (c) the number of unknowns (intelligence gaps drive collection costs); (d) the depth of analysis required (formal IPB products are more costly than abbreviated analysis). At battalion level, IPB costs hours to a day. At theater level, IPB costs weeks to months. In the LLM-agent context, IPB-equivalent analysis costs tokens proportional to: the size of the codebase to analyze, the depth of dependency mapping, and the number of potential obstacles/threats to model.
replanning_built_in: IPB is designed for continuous update, not one-time production. The doctrine explicitly states that IPB is 'a continuous process' — it does not stop when planning ends but continues through execution. When the situation changes (new intelligence, unexpected enemy action, weather change, civil incident), IPB products are updated and the refined understanding feeds back into mission analysis and COA assessment. This continuous IPB is what enables responsive replanning: the commander bases replanning decisions on current IPB, not on the initial pre-operation analysis. The continuous nature distinguishes IPB from a static intelligence estimate.
trigger_mechanism: IPB update triggers are primarily intelligence-driven: (a) PIR answers — when a Priority Intelligence Requirement is satisfied or updated; (b) significant enemy activity — enemy actions that deviate from or confirm predicted COAs; (c) environmental change — weather, civil situation, or terrain modification; (d) new collection capabilities — sensors or reconnaissance assets become available; (e) phase transitions — each operational phase triggers an IPB review; (f) commander's direction — the commander orders re-analysis of specific aspects. During execution, combat information (spot reports, sensor feeds) provides the continuous stream of triggers for IPB updates.
adaptation_cost: Partial IPB update (refining one aspect based on new intelligence) has low cost — the S2 updates the relevant product and briefs the change. Full IPB re-analysis (the situation has changed fundamentally) has high cost — the equivalent of the original IPB effort but compressed into available execution time. The IPB product architecture supports partial updates: individual overlays, templates, and matrices can be updated without redoing the entire analysis. The DST is specifically designed for easy update during execution. In the LLM-agent context, partial updates map to incremental context updates with low token cost; full re-analysis maps to re-processing the entire environment model.
failure_recovery_mechanism: When the IPB's predictions fail (the enemy acts differently than expected or the terrain effects were misjudged), recovery involves: (a) immediate IPB re-analysis — what did we get wrong and why?; (b) emergency intelligence collection focused on the discrepancy; (c) updating threat COA models based on observed enemy behavior; (d) adjusting the plan based on the updated understanding. The event template and DST provide the baseline for detecting that IPB has failed — when events occur at unexpected times or locations, or when decision point conditions are met differently than predicted, the IPB is flagged for re-evaluation. Recovery speed depends on how quickly the discrepancy is recognized and how quickly new intelligence can be collected and analyzed.
output_artifacts: IPB produces a comprehensive suite of intelligence products. Primary products: (1) Modified Combined Obstacles Overlay (MCOO) — depicts terrain constraints on maneuver. (2) Weather Effects Matrix — maps weather factors to specific operational impacts. (3) Threat Doctrinal Templates — graphic depictions of enemy deployment and operations without terrain constraints. (4) Situation Template (SITTEMP) — how the enemy will deploy and operate WITH terrain/weather constraints. (5) Event Template — time-phased prediction of critical events and target appearances, keyed to NAI/TAIs. (6) Decision Support Template (DST) — decision points keyed to battlefield events. (7) High-Value Target (HVT) List — enemy assets critical to mission success. (8) Intelligence Requirements — including PIRs and information requirements. (9) Enemy COA statements and sketches — narrative and graphic description of most likely and most dangerous COAs. Supporting products: running estimates, intelligence summaries, and collection plans.
briefing_format: IPB is briefed as part of the Mission Analysis Brief. The S2 walks through: (a) the operational environment (AO, AI, significant characteristics); (b) terrain analysis (MCOO, key terrain, avenues of approach, engagement areas); (c) weather effects (current and forecast, impact on friendly and enemy operations); (d) threat evaluation (order of battle, capabilities, recent activity); (e) enemy COAs (most likely and most dangerous, with SITTEMP and event template overlays); (f) intelligence gaps and PIR recommendations. The briefing is map-centric: IPB products are graphic overlays that the commander and staff can see simultaneously, enabling rapid comprehension of spatial relationships. In the LLM-agent context, this maps to: a structured environment model (codebase map, dependency graph, obstacle register) presented in both human-readable summary and machine-parseable format.
traceability: IPB products are built with traceability in mind: each analytical judgment should be traceable to its source intelligence. The S2's running estimate documents the evidence chain. However, the quality of traceability depends on staff discipline — source citations are often incomplete under time pressure. The JIPOE variant emphasizes structured documentation because joint and multinational partners need to understand the basis for intelligence assessments. Key assumptions are explicitly documented in IPB products so that when assumptions are invalidated, all dependent analyses can be identified and updated.
agent_communication_protocol: Not applicable in original context. In the LLM-agent context, IPB-equivalent environment analysis would be communicated as a structured environment model: (a) the codebase map (terrain equivalent) — a dependency graph with architecture, key modules, interfaces; (b) the obstacle register (enemy equivalent) — known bugs, technical debt, integration challenges, rate limits; (c) the environmental conditions (weather equivalent) — deployment environment, OS constraints, library versions, CI/CD status; (d) the threat model (enemy COA equivalent) — predicted failure modes, integration conflicts, performance bottlenecks. This model serves as the shared understanding baseline for all agents in the JORDAN pipeline.
learning_memory_architecture: IPB has institutional learning through doctrine updates (FM 2-01.3 revisions) and intelligence lessons learned programs. At the unit level, IPB products for a specific area of operations are maintained and updated over time — a division that operates repeatedly in the same area builds an increasingly refined IPB baseline. However, there is no systematic mechanism for transferring IPB models between units rotating through the same area — the new unit often starts fresh. In the LLM-agent context, IPB-equivalent environment models should be stored and retrieved: when JORDAN works on a codebase it has analyzed before, the previous codebase map, dependency graph, and obstacle register are loaded as the starting point, reducing the cost of re-analysis.
latency_bounds: IPB latency depends on echelon and available time. Battalion: 1-6 hours for abbreviated IPB. Brigade: 6-24 hours for deliberate IPB. Division/Corps: 24-72 hours for full IPB with all products. Joint/theater: days to weeks for comprehensive JIPOE. The process degrades under time pressure by: (a) abbreviating products (MCOO only, single enemy COA); (b) relying on existing databases rather than new collection; (c) using doctrinal templates without terrain-specific refinement. The minimum viable IPB is the commander's personal terrain walk and threat estimate — minutes to hours — but this is reconnaissance, not formal IPB. In the LLM-agent context, IPB-equivalent analysis latency would range from seconds (cached codebase map retrieval) to minutes (full dependency graph generation and obstacle analysis).
evaluation_benchmarking: IPB is evaluated through: (a) training center assessments — observer-controllers evaluate IPB quality during NTC, JRTC, JMRC rotations; (b) operational outcomes — did the IPB correctly predict enemy COAs and environmental effects?; (c) intelligence after-action reviews — comparing IPB predictions to actual enemy behavior. There is no formal 'IPB-bench' — evaluation is qualitative and experiential. The US Army Intelligence Center conducts periodic assessments of IPB training and doctrine effectiveness.
mapping_strength: Strong — IPB maps directly to systematic environment analysis for software development and LLM agent workflows. The mapping: (1) Define the Battlefield Environment maps to defining the problem scope, codebase boundaries, and relevant systems. (2) Describe the Battlefield's Effects maps to analyzing the codebase architecture (terrain equivalent), dependency constraints, test environment, and CI/CD pipeline (weather equivalent). (3) Evaluate the Threat maps to identifying obstacles — technical debt, known bugs, fragile dependencies, integration challenges, API rate limits. (4) Determine Threat Courses of Action maps to predicting failure modes — what will break if we change X? Where are the cascading effects most likely? What's the worst-case scenario? What's the most likely obstacle course? (5) IPB products (MCOO, SITTEMP, event template, DST) map to: codebase dependency graph (MCOO), obstacle/failure mode registry (SITTEMP), predicted integration failure timeline (event template), and decision points for replanning (DST). What transfers directly: the four-step systematic approach, the emphasis on understanding the environment before planning, the distinction between the most likely and most dangerous scenarios, and the continuous update principle. What needs translation: military terrain analysis (OCOKA) must be replaced with codebase architecture analysis patterns; threat modeling (enemy as thinking adversary) must be reframed as obstacle/failure mode modeling; the spatial analysis focus must be generalized to dependency analysis.
gaps: Critical gaps for software/LLM agent application: (1) Adversary assumption — IPB models the enemy as a thinking adversary; software obstacles (bugs, complexity, dependency conflicts) do not think, adapt, or deceive (though security threats do). (2) Terrain analysis framework is military-specific — OCOKA has no direct software analog and must be replaced with codebase analysis patterns. (3) Static analysis bias — IPB products are relatively static representations that are updated periodically; software environments can change in real time (new commits, dependency updates) and the analysis cycle may not keep pace. (4) Assumes dedicated intelligence support — IPB is conducted by a specialized intelligence staff; agent systems must perform their own environment analysis without a dedicated 'intelligence agent' (unless one is designed into the system). (5) Does not address non-deterministic environments — IPB models a physical environment where terrain and weather follow predictable patterns; software environments (cloud infrastructure, network conditions, user behavior) can be non-deterministic. (6) IPB's threat COA development is labor-intensive — modeling adversary options requires deep domain expertise that LLM agents may lack. (7) Products are human-consumable graphics — they need translation to machine-parseable structured data for agent consumption.
adaptation_required: To apply IPB principles to the JORDAN pipeline: (1) Implement an Environment Analysis node in the JORDAN research phase that follows a four-step IPB-like structure: Define Scope -> Analyze Environment -> Identify Obstacles -> Predict Failure Modes. (2) Replace OCOKA with a software terrain analysis framework: Architecture Map (system components and their relationships), Dependency Graph (what depends on what), Constraint Register (API limits, performance boundaries, compatibility requirements), Change Impact Map (what changes affect which components). (3) Replace enemy doctrinal templates with obstacle templates: common bug patterns, known anti-patterns, dependency conflict scenarios, integration failure modes. (4) Replace threat COA development with failure mode prediction: what are the most likely obstacles (most likely COA) and what are the most dangerous failure modes (most dangerous COA)? (5) Implement continuous environment monitoring — the Environment Analysis node re-runs when significant changes are detected (new commits, test failures, dependency updates). (6) Produce machine-parseable environment products: a structured environment model (YAML/JSON) with dependency graph, obstacle register, failure mode registry, and decision points — this serves as the shared context for all JORDAN agents. (7) Store and retrieve environment models: when JORDAN re-works a codebase, load the previous environment model as the starting point and incrementally update it. (8) Integrate with METT-TC-equivalent context assessment: the IPB-equivalent environment analysis provides the detailed 'terrain' and 'enemy' analysis that feeds the broader METT-TC-style task context assessment.
validation_context: Validated through decades of military operations since WWII. Combat Training Center rotations (NTC, JRTC, JMRC) where IPB quality is formally assessed by observer-controllers in every rotation. Operation Desert Storm (1991): IPB correctly predicted Iraqi defensive dispositions and identified the western flank avenue of approach that enabled the 'left hook' maneuver. Operation Iraqi Freedom (2003): IPB successfully modeled Iraqi conventional forces (Phase III) but underestimated the complexity of civil considerations and the emerging insurgency (Phase IV) — leading to the doctrinal expansion from IPB to JIPOE with PMESII. Counterinsurgency operations (Iraq, Afghanistan): IPB was adapted to incorporate population-centric analysis (human terrain mapping, tribal dynamics) — revealing limitations of the original enemy-and-terrain focus. Joint operations: JIPOE validated through NATO and coalition operations. Professional military education: IPB is foundational curriculum at all military intelligence courses.
known_failures: (1) Assumption lock-in: IPB assumptions become treated as facts, and when new intelligence contradicts them, the IPB is not updated — 'the IPB said the enemy would defend here, and we're sticking to it despite evidence they've moved.' (2) Template over-reliance: Threat COA development based on doctrinal templates fails when the enemy does something non-doctrinal — a particular problem against adaptive or unconventional adversaries. (3) Terrain fixation: The IPB's terrain-and-enemy focus can crowd out civil considerations, weather effects, and other environmental factors. (4) Time-compression failure: When planning time is inadequate, IPB is abbreviated to the point of uselessness — terrain is glanced at, enemy is assumed to follow doctrine, and the resulting plan is built on superficial analysis. (5) Echelon misalignment: Higher echelon IPB products are too coarse for subordinate units, which must redo analysis at their level — but time pressure often causes subordinates to use higher's products uncritically. (6) Intel-staff siloing: IPB is the S2's process, and other staff sections (S3, engineer, civil affairs) contribute peripherally rather than as full partners — the integration of terrain, threat, and civil factors is incomplete. (7) Over-classification: IPB products at higher classifications cannot be shared with coalition partners or subordinate units that lack clearance, fragmenting the common understanding that IPB is supposed to create. (8) Weather neglect: Weather analysis is consistently the least thorough component of IPB — terrain and threat dominate staff attention, and weather effects are treated superficially despite their operational significance. (9) Collection-IPB disconnect: IPB identifies intelligence gaps but the collection system may not be able to fill them in time, leaving IPB products based on assumptions that could have been resolved. (10) Static DST: The Decision Support Template is produced during planning and not updated during execution, so decision points based on stale intelligence trigger incorrect decisions.
where_still_used: US Army — IPB is foundational doctrine taught at all intelligence courses (Fort Huachuca) and applied at every echelon. US Marine Corps — IPB within the MCPP intelligence preparation framework. US Navy and Air Force — domain-specific IPB variants (maritime IPB, air IPB). Joint commands — JIPOE (Joint Intelligence Preparation of the Operational Environment) is JP 2-01.3 doctrine, applied across all combatant commands. NATO — IPB methodology embedded in NATO intelligence doctrine and the Comprehensive Operations Planning Directive (COPD). Allied nations — UK (Joint Operational Intelligence Preparation), Australia (JIPOE), Canada (CFJP 2.0), all use IPB or IPB-derived methodology. Law enforcement and homeland security — IPB methodology adapted for threat assessment and critical infrastructure protection. The framework's systematic decomposition of environment and threat has influenced intelligence analysis methodology well beyond the military.
